question_title: Will the United States Department of Justice indict the Federal Reserve before May 2026?
summary: The DOJ's investigation into the Federal Reserve/Jerome Powell has been effectively blocked by federal courts. Subpoenas were quashed March 13 and reconsideration denied April 3. DOJ admitted having no evidence of a crime. An appeal to the D.C. Circuit would take months, far beyond the May 1 deadline. Filing an indictment without evidence and against judicial opposition is practically impossible in the remaining ~27 days.

factors.description: Subpoenas quashed by federal judge twice (March 13 and April 3, 2026), blocking DOJ's evidence gathering
DOJ prosecutor admitted having no evidence that Powell committed a crime
Only ~27 days remain until May 1, 2026 deadline - insufficient time for appeal, evidence gathering, and indictment
D.C. Circuit appeals process typically takes months, not weeks
Indicting the Federal Reserve or its chair would be completely unprecedented in US history
Political incentive exists for Trump/DOJ to escalate pressure on Powell before his term ends May 15
Senator Tillis blocking Warsh nomination creates counterincentive against continued investigation

reasoning:
The evidence is overwhelming that an indictment before May 1, 2026 is extremely unlikely. Here's my assessment:

**Key findings:**
- Judge Boasberg quashed DOJ subpoenas on March 13 and denied reconsideration on April 3 — both rulings found the probe was pretextual political pressure, not a legitimate investigation
- DOJ's own prosecutor admitted they "do not currently know what evidence exists that Powell committed a crime"
- Only ~27 days remain before the May 1 deadline
- DOJ plans to appeal to the D.C. Circuit, but appellate proceedings take months
- No federal criminal indictment of the Fed or its chair has ever occurred in US history
- Senator Tillis is blocking Trump's preferred Fed chair replacement (Warsh) specifically because of this investigation, creating political costs for escalation

For YES to occur, DOJ would need to either file charges without evidence (extraordinary prosecutorial misconduct requiring grand jury cooperation despite a skeptical supervising judge) or find an entirely new legal avenue in under a month. Neither is plausible.

resolution_criteria: The question will resolve as **Yes** if, at any time before May 1, 2026, the United States Department of Justice (DOJ) files a federal criminal indictment against the Federal Reserve or Jerome Powell.
fine_print:
This question will resolve according to information published by [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions), including [the DOJ Press Release news webpage](https://www.justice.gov/news/press-releases).
